name: TruthMirror
oneLiner: A multi-agent system that fact-checks geopolitical claims against real sources in real time.
problem: Most news consumers have no fast way to check whether a geopolitical claim is actually supported by primary sources, or whether it's one outlet's framing dressed up as fact.
approach: I built a multi-agent pipeline using local and cloud LLMs together — a routing agent decides which claims need deep verification, a retrieval agent pulls from Wikipedia and live news sources, and a synthesis agent triangulates across them before producing a verdict. Version 2 introduced a temporal scope gate to stop the system from treating outdated sources as current evidence, and a logging layer that records exactly which sources were used for every verdict.
outcome: The v2.1 patch fixed source-routing bugs and added the temporal gate, making verdicts noticeably more reliable on fast-moving stories. The hardest lesson was that retrieval quality matters more than model size — a smaller model with better sources consistently beat a bigger model with sloppy retrieval.

name: OnlineSchool
oneLiner: A full LMS built on WordPress, handling courses, payments, and interactive lessons end to end.
problem: The client needed a complete teaching platform — course delivery, payment handling, and genuinely interactive lessons — without the cost or rigidity of a custom-built LMS from scratch.
approach: I combined LearnPress for course structure, WooCommerce for payments, and Elementor Pro for the front-end, then layered in custom PHP and ACF Pro fields where the off-the-shelf plugins couldn't do what was needed. For lesson interactivity, I built and embedded custom H5P widgets rather than relying on static video.
outcome: The platform handles real enrolled students and real payments. The biggest takeaway was learning where to draw the line between configuring existing tools versus writing custom code — over-customizing a CMS creates maintenance debt fast.

name: ColorMixer
oneLiner: An interactive additive-color teaching widget, built to make RGB light mixing intuitive for young learners.
problem: Color mixing is taught with static diagrams that don't convey why red and green light make yellow — the concept needs to be felt, not just read.
approach: I built a self-contained draggable widget where three colored light circles can be dragged over each other, using mix-blend-mode: screen to genuinely simulate additive light mixing in the browser rather than faking it with pre-rendered colors. It was packaged as a single embeddable file for direct use inside H5P lessons.
outcome: Students could immediately see and manipulate the relationship between light colors instead of memorizing it. It also taught me how much can be achieved with a single well-chosen CSS property instead of a heavier canvas-based simulation.
